heroOverview: Cough is the most common reason for visiting a primary care clinician in the United States, generating more than 30 million clinic visits annually. It is a forceful, coordinated respiratory reflex that clears the airways of mucus, irritants, and foreign material, triggered by sensory nerves lining the throat, airways, and even the ear canal. Acute cough lasting fewer than three weeks is overwhelmingly caused by upper respiratory viral infections; subacute and chronic cough — defined as persisting three to eight weeks, and beyond eight weeks, respectively — have a narrower differential dominated in adults by postnasal drip, asthma, and acid reflux. While cough is usually a nuisance, it can signal serious underlying disease ranging from pneumonia to lung cancer, and a persistent cough lasting more than eight weeks always warrants clinical evaluation to identify its cause.

keyStats.prevalence: Chronic cough affects roughly 10-15% of adults worldwide; acute cough accounts for more than 30 million U.S. primary care visits per year
keyStats.demographics: Slightly more common in women, who have a lower cough threshold due to hormonal and airway differences; more frequent in smokers and older adults
keyStats.avgAge: Chronic cough peaks in adults aged 40-70; acute cough in upper respiratory infections is universal across all ages
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 10-15% of the global adult population experiences chronic cough at any given time

definition: Cough (ICD-10: R05) is a sudden, forceful expulsion of air from the lungs produced by coordinated contraction of the thoracic, abdominal, and laryngeal muscles, mediated by afferent signals from cough receptors in the larynx, trachea, bronchi, and peripheral airways. It is classified by duration: acute (less than 3 weeks), subacute (3 to 8 weeks), and chronic (more than 8 weeks). The mechanism involves an initial deep inspiration, glottic closure with breath-holding to build intrathoracic pressure, then explosive glottic opening with rapid airflow that can exceed 500 km/h. Cough serves a vital protective function — clearing secretions and preventing aspiration — but becomes pathological when the cough reflex is sensitised or when excessive mucus is produced. In primary care, the upper-airway cough syndrome (postnasal drip), cough-variant asthma, and gastro-oesophageal reflux disease account for more than 90% of chronic cough cases. Investigations are guided by whether the cough is productive or dry, by the duration, and by accompanying features such as haemoptysis, weight loss, or dyspnoea. The condition falls under general medicine for initial assessment and may involve pulmonology, gastroenterology, or otolaryngology depending on cause.

typesClassification.description: Usually due to viral upper respiratory tract infection, the common cold, or influenza; typically self-limiting and requires no specific treatment beyond symptomatic care.
Often a post-infectious cough that lingers after a respiratory illness, or an evolving bacterial sinusitis or pertussis; most resolve spontaneously but may respond to targeted treatment.
In non-smokers who are not taking an ACE inhibitor, the three commonest causes are upper-airway cough syndrome, cough-variant asthma, and gastro-oesophageal reflux; full evaluation is needed.
Associated with increased mucus production in bronchitis, pneumonia, COPD, and bronchiectasis; the colour and quantity of sputum guide management.
Characteristic of viral infections, ACE-inhibitor drug effect, interstitial lung disease, and cough-variant asthma; absence of mucus does not mean the airways are unaffected.

Chronic cough that persists despite treatment of identified triggers; represents a hypersensitised cough reflex and is increasingly managed with neuromodulatory therapies such as gabapentin or gefapixant.

primarySymptoms: A forceful expulsion of air from the lungs, which may be wet and mucus-producing or dry and non-productive, representing the cardinal feature of the condition.
Sore or raw throat caused by repeated trauma to the pharyngeal and laryngeal mucosa during prolonged coughing episodes.
Hoarseness or a changed voice quality, resulting from vocal cord irritation during high-velocity cough.
Chest tightness or an aching sensation in the chest wall and intercostal muscles from repeated forceful contractions.
Post-cough urinary incontinence, particularly in women, caused by sudden spikes in intra-abdominal pressure during cough.
Sleep disruption due to nocturnal coughing episodes, causing daytime fatigue, reduced concentration, and reduced quality of life.
Headache or facial pressure accompanying sinus-related cough, reflecting congestion or sinusitis as an underlying trigger.
Nausea or vomiting in severe paroxysmal coughing fits, particularly characteristic of pertussis.
Rib pain or costal cartilage tenderness from repeated cough-induced strain, occasionally leading to rib fractures in osteoporotic patients.
An ongoing sensation of mucus dripping down the back of the throat (postnasal drip), commonly an associated symptom and a leading cause of chronic cough.
earlyWarningSigns: A dry tickle or irritation at the back of the throat or upper chest that triggers brief, frequent coughing episodes.
A slight increase in mucus production or throat-clearing that precedes a frank productive cough.
A persistent need to clear the throat repeatedly, which may represent early postnasal drip.
New cough arising within a week or two of starting an ACE inhibitor such as lisinopril or ramipril.
emergencySigns: Coughing up more than a teaspoon of blood (haemoptysis) or finding blood-streaked sputum, which requires same-day evaluation.
High fever (above 38.5°C), rapid breathing, and pleuritic chest pain suggesting pneumonia or pulmonary embolism.
Severe breathing difficulty, stridor, or cyanosis (blue discolouration of lips or fingertips) indicating acute airway compromise.
Sudden choking in a child or adult after eating, which may signal foreign body aspiration requiring immediate emergency care.
Unexplained significant weight loss combined with persistent cough lasting more than eight weeks, raising concern for lung cancer or tuberculosis.

causes.description: Rhinoviruses, coronaviruses, and influenza viruses infect the upper airway epithelium, triggering mucosal inflammation and sensitising cough receptors. The resulting cough typically lasts one to three weeks and is the most common cause of acute cough globally.

Mucus dripping from inflamed nasal or sinus mucosa onto the posterior pharynx stimulates cough receptors directly. It is the leading identifiable cause of chronic cough, accounting for an estimated 20-40% of cases in large clinical series.

In this form of asthma, bronchoconstriction and airway inflammation lower the cough threshold without causing obvious wheeze or dyspnoea. It accounts for 25-30% of chronic cough referrals and responds to standard asthma therapy including inhaled corticosteroids.

Acid or non-acid reflux into the oesophagus and proximal airway stimulates afferent cough receptors via a vagally mediated reflex. Reflux is responsible for roughly 20-40% of chronic cough cases, and up to half of these patients have no heartburn symptoms.

Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors cause a dry, persistent cough in 10-15% of users by accumulating kinins in the respiratory tract that sensitise cough receptors. The cough typically begins within weeks of starting the drug and resolves one to four weeks after stopping it.

Bacterial infection produces purulent mucus and severe airway inflammation; productive cough lasting more than two weeks with fever and systemic illness suggests bacterial pneumonia, which requires antibiotic treatment and sometimes hospitalisation.

Bordetella pertussis produces a distinctive paroxysmal cough with an inspiratory whoop, vomiting, and post-tussive exhaustion that can persist for weeks; it can affect unimmunised children and incompletely immunised adults.

causes.cause: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and smoking
causes.description: Cigarette smoke chronically irritates airway epithelium and impairs mucociliary clearance, generating a daily productive cough — so-called smoker's cough — that affects roughly 40-50% of long-term smokers and indicates established airway disease.

riskFactors.description: The leading risk factor for chronic productive cough; tobacco smoke paralyses cilia, increases mucus production, and chronically inflames the bronchial epithelium. Smoking cessation reduces cough frequency significantly within weeks.

Approximately 10-15% of patients prescribed lisinopril, ramipril, or other ACE inhibitors develop a persistent dry cough; switching to an angiotensin receptor blocker resolves it without sacrificing blood-pressure or cardioprotective benefit.
Asthmatic airways are chronically hyperresponsive; cough-variant asthma is more common in atopic individuals. Poorly controlled asthma is the second most common cause of chronic cough in adults.

Reflux triggers a vagally mediated cough reflex; risk rises with obesity, high-fat diet, alcohol, and lying flat. Treating reflux with proton pump inhibitors and lifestyle changes reduces cough in 70-85% of GERD-related cases.

Dusty workplaces, chemical fumes, indoor air pollution, and dry air all irritate the airway mucosa; workers in construction, mining, and manufacturing have two- to three-fold higher rates of occupational cough.

Women have a lower cough-reflex threshold than men, partly attributed to hormonal modulation of cough-receptor sensitivity; women are twice as likely to present with chronic cough in specialist clinics.

HIV infection, chemotherapy, organ transplantation, and high-dose steroids impair mucosal immune defences, increasing susceptibility to recurrent respiratory infections and opportunistic pathogens that cause persistent cough.

Low-humidity air in pressurised aircraft cabins and heated buildings dries the airway epithelium, reduces mucociliary clearance, and can trigger or worsen cough, particularly in individuals with baseline airway sensitivity.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 40-70, the peak age group for chronic cough and COPD-related cough
Current and former smokers of any age
Women, who have a lower cough threshold and make up the majority of chronic cough clinic referrals
Children aged 1-5, in whom viral respiratory infections and pertussis are the commonest causes
Patients on ACE inhibitor therapy for hypertension or heart failure
Individuals with asthma, GERD, or allergic rhinitis
Occupationally exposed workers in dusty or chemical environments
diagnosisOverview: The evaluation of cough begins with a thorough history that classifies duration, character (wet versus dry), timing (nocturnal, post-prandial, on exertion), and associated features such as wheeze, heartburn, haemoptysis, weight loss, or recent travel. A medication history is essential: ACE inhibitor cough accounts for up to 10% of chronic cough presentations and the diagnosis is made empirically by stopping the drug. Physical examination assesses the upper airway, chest, and cardiovascular system. In acute cough, chest X-ray is reserved for those with fever, tachycardia, reduced oxygen saturation, or signs of consolidation. For chronic cough in a non-smoker not on an ACE inhibitor, the empirical step-up approach recommended by CHEST guidelines tests for and treats the three commonest causes sequentially: first upper-airway cough syndrome, then cough-variant asthma (with a spirometry and bronchodilator reversibility or bronchial challenge test), and then GERD (with a proton pump inhibitor trial). CT of the chest is indicated when the plain X-ray is abnormal, when there is haemoptysis or weight loss, or when an occupational exposure raises concern for malignancy or interstitial lung disease. Sputum culture and bronchoscopy are reserved for suspected infection, malignancy, or foreign body. The differential also includes pertussis (confirmed by nasopharyngeal PCR), obstructive sleep apnoea, and, increasingly recognised, refractory chronic cough with cough hypersensitivity syndrome.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging to exclude pneumonia, malignancy, pleural effusion, cardiac enlargement, or other structural causes of cough.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You stand in front of an X-ray machine and hold your breath briefly; the study takes less than five minutes and delivers minimal radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures airflow obstruction and determines whether it reverses with a bronchodilator, supporting a diagnosis of asthma or COPD as the cough cause.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You breathe forcefully into a mouthpiece connected to a device that plots your flow-volume curve; the test takes 20-30 minutes including a wait after inhaling salbutamol.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects airway hyperresponsiveness when spirometry is normal but cough-variant asthma is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You inhale increasing doses of a provocation agent; the test is supervised by a trained technician, takes about 45 minutes, and is stopped if wheeze or a 20% drop in FEV1 occurs.

diagnosticTests.test: CT of the chest and sinuses
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates the lungs for malignancy, interstitial disease, bronchiectasis, and the sinuses for chronic rhinosinusitis when first-line tests are non-diagnostic.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in a CT scanner; the scan takes a few minutes and may involve iodinated contrast injection depending on clinical indication.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The definitive test for GERD-related cough; correlates acid or non-acid reflux episodes with cough events when a PPI trial has not resolved the cough.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin catheter is placed through the nose into the oesophagus and worn for 24 hours with a recording device; it is uncomfortable but not painful.

diagnosticTests.test: Nasopharyngeal swab PCR for Bordetella pertussis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms pertussis in patients with paroxysmal cough lasting more than two weeks, especially those in contact with unimmunised individuals.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab is passed into the nasopharynx — briefly uncomfortable — and sent for PCR; results return in 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies bacterial pathogens in productive cough and screens for malignant cells in patients with risk factors for lung cancer.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You cough deeply into a sterile container first thing in the morning on one to three consecutive days; no preparation is needed.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of cough is directed at the underlying cause rather than the cough reflex itself wherever possible. Acute viral cough is managed supportively with honey (proven superior to placebo in children over one year), adequate hydration, and steam inhalation; over-the-counter suppressants provide modest, short-term relief. Subacute post-infectious cough usually resolves within eight weeks without specific treatment, though a short course of inhaled ipratropium may accelerate resolution. For chronic cough, empirical treatment in the sequence recommended by the CHEST and ERS guidelines begins with intranasal corticosteroids and antihistamines for upper-airway cough syndrome; if the cough does not improve within four to eight weeks, an eight-week trial of a proton pump inhibitor plus dietary modification targets GERD, and an inhaled corticosteroid (with or without a long-acting beta-agonist) addresses cough-variant asthma. ACE inhibitor cough resolves predictably when the offending drug is replaced by an angiotensin receptor blocker. Refractory chronic cough — cough that persists despite treatment of all identified causes — now has approved pharmacological options: the P2X3 receptor antagonist gefapixant (approved in Europe and Japan) reduced cough frequency by approximately 40% versus placebo at the 45 mg twice-daily dose in the COUGH-1 and COUGH-2 trials. Neuromodulators including gabapentin and pregabalin have evidence in refractory cases but carry side-effect burdens. Cough-suppressant physiotherapy (breathing exercises and laryngeal hygiene manoeuvres) is recommended as an adjunct in all chronic cough patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Intranasal corticosteroids (e.g. fluticasone, mometasone)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for upper-airway cough syndrome due to allergic rhinitis or non-allergic rhinitis; delivered directly to the nasal mucosa to reduce inflammation and postnasal drip.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces chronic cough associated with rhinitis in approximately 60-75% of patients within 4-6 weeks of consistent use.

medicalTreatments.name: Proton pump inhibitors (e.g. omeprazole, lansoprazole)
medicalTreatments.description: Suppress gastric acid production to reduce reflux-triggered cough; most effective when taken 30-60 minutes before meals twice daily for a minimum of eight weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolves or substantially reduces GERD-related cough in approximately 70-80% of patients who take the full course as directed.

medicalTreatments.name: Inhaled corticosteroids (e.g. budesonide, beclomethasone)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce airway eosinophilic inflammation in cough-variant asthma; given via inhaler once or twice daily, usually combined with a beta-agonist bronchodilator in moderate disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves meaningful cough reduction in roughly 80% of patients with cough-variant asthma when used consistently for 4-8 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Gefapixant (P2X3 receptor antagonist)
medicalTreatments.description: The first approved cough-specific therapy for refractory or unexplained chronic cough; blocks ATP-mediated activation of cough receptors in the airway epithelium. Dose-limiting taste disturbance (dysgeusia) affects approximately 40% of users at the 45 mg dose.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduced cough frequency by approximately 40% compared to placebo in phase 3 trials enrolling approximately 2,000 patients (COUGH-1 and COUGH-2, NEJM 2022).

medicalTreatments.name: Antihistamines (first-generation, e.g. diphenhydramine, chlorphenamine)
medicalTreatments.description: Used in upper-airway cough syndrome; first-generation antihistamines have a modest additional drying effect on nasal secretions. Newer non-sedating antihistamines are preferred for long-term use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined with a decongestant, reduce chronic cough due to postnasal drip by approximately 40-50% in clinical trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Gabapentin and pregabalin
medicalTreatments.description: Neuromodulators that dampen central and peripheral cough pathway sensitisation in refractory chronic cough; used off-label when other treatments have failed, starting at low doses and titrating.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Gabapentin reduced cough-specific quality-of-life scores by approximately 40% versus placebo in a 2012 randomised trial, with improvement in roughly 50% of refractory cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Honey
alternativeTreatments.description: A single dose of honey (2.5-5 mL) at bedtime has been shown in multiple randomised trials to reduce nocturnal cough and improve sleep in children aged over one year at least as well as dextromethorphan; do not give to infants under 12 months due to botulism risk.

alternativeTreatments.name: Steam inhalation and humidification
alternativeTreatments.description: Warm moist air soothes irritated upper airway mucosa and liquefies secretions; widely used for symptomatic relief of viral cough, although RCT evidence is limited. A face mask over a bowl of hot water or a shower achieves a similar effect.

alternativeTreatments.name: Speech pathology cough suppression therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: A structured programme of breathing exercises, laryngeal hygiene, and cough suppression manoeuvres that retrain the cough reflex; shown in randomised trials to reduce cough frequency and improve quality of life in chronic cough, complementing pharmacological treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: A cough lasting more than eight weeks should be evaluated by a clinician, even without alarming features. Haemoptysis, unexplained weight loss, or a chronic cough in a current or former smoker warrant urgent specialist assessment — typically pulmonology — to exclude malignancy. Gastroenterology is appropriate if GERD is suspected and does not respond to empirical treatment, and otolaryngology is indicated when chronic rhinosinusitis or laryngeal pathology is suspected. Refractory chronic cough that fails standard empirical treatment should be referred to a dedicated cough clinic where available.
doctorSelectionGuide: For chronic cough unresponsive to two to three empirical treatment trials, seek a pulmonologist or a clinician with a specific interest in cough. Useful questions to ask include whether the practice uses 24-hour pH-impedance testing, whether they have access to bronchial challenge testing, and what their approach is to refractory cough including cough physiotherapy and neuromodulatory agents. If an ENT cause is suspected, a combined pulmonology and otolaryngology assessment is ideal.
preventionStrategies: Stop smoking; smoking cessation is the single most effective preventive and therapeutic intervention for chronic productive cough.
Treat underlying asthma, allergic rhinitis, and GERD proactively with maintenance therapy to prevent cough flares.
Stay up to date with pertussis vaccination (Tdap booster) and annual influenza vaccination.
If started on an ACE inhibitor and a persistent cough develops within weeks, ask your clinician about switching to an angiotensin receptor blocker.
Minimise occupational dust and fume exposure with appropriate respiratory protection and adequate ventilation.
Maintain good hand hygiene during viral respiratory infection seasons to reduce acute infectious cough.
lifestyleModifications: Elevate the head of the bed by 15-20 cm and avoid eating within two to three hours of lying down to reduce nocturnal GERD-related cough.
Stay well hydrated; adequate fluid intake keeps airway secretions thinner and easier to clear.
Avoid exposure to strong perfumes, cleaning chemicals, and air fresheners that sensitise the cough reflex.
Keep indoor humidity at 40-50%; very dry air irritates airway mucosa and worsens cough.
Practice nasal saline irrigation daily if postnasal drip is contributing to cough.
Reduce alcohol and caffeine, both of which relax the lower oesophageal sphincter and worsen reflux-driven cough.

recommended: Warm liquids such as broth or herbal teas, which soothe irritated airways and thin mucus
Honey added to warm water or tea as a natural cough suppressant for those over one year
High-fibre diet to maintain healthy gastrointestinal motility and reduce reflux
Small, frequent meals to minimise gastric distension and reflux triggering
avoid: Acidic foods (citrus, tomatoes, vinegar) and spicy meals if GERD is contributing to cough
High-fat meals, chocolate, and peppermint, which relax the lower oesophageal sphincter
Alcohol and caffeinated beverages that worsen reflux and dry airway mucosa
Eating large meals within two to three hours of bedtime

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise improves respiratory muscle strength and mucociliary clearance, and is encouraged for most people with chronic cough. During acute respiratory infections, reduce intensity and rest if feverish. For exercise-induced cough or suspected exercise-triggered asthma, pre-treatment with an inhaled short-acting bronchodilator (salbutamol) 15 minutes before exercise and warming up gradually can prevent symptoms. Swimming in a warm, humid environment is often better tolerated than outdoor cold-air exercise.
dailyManagement: Use prescribed intranasal corticosteroids or asthma inhalers consistently, not only when symptoms worsen.
Perform nasal saline irrigation each morning to clear postnasal secretions before they trigger daytime cough.
Keep a cough diary to identify triggering foods, postures, or exposures that can then be modified.
Avoid throat-clearing, which maintains laryngeal irritation; swallow hard or take a sip of water instead.
Complete the full course of any antibiotic prescribed for a bacterial respiratory infection.
prognosis: The prognosis of cough depends entirely on its cause. Acute viral cough resolves in one to three weeks in the vast majority of people. Post-infectious subacute cough clears within eight weeks without treatment in roughly 85% of cases. Chronic cough due to identifiable and treatable causes — postnasal drip, asthma, or GERD — responds to targeted therapy in 70-90% of patients when the treatment is taken consistently. ACE inhibitor cough resolves in virtually all patients within four weeks of stopping the drug. Refractory chronic cough with cough hypersensitivity syndrome is more challenging; gefapixant and neuromodulators reduce cough frequency by approximately 40% compared to placebo, providing meaningful quality-of-life benefit for the roughly 40% of chronic cough patients who do not respond to standard care. Cough due to serious underlying conditions such as lung cancer or pulmonary fibrosis is managed alongside the primary disease and may persist even with optimal treatment.
recoveryTimeline: Acute viral cough typically peaks at two to three days and resolves within one to three weeks. Subacute post-infectious cough resolves in three to eight weeks. Chronic cough due to upper-airway cough syndrome improves in two to four weeks of intranasal corticosteroid therapy; GERD-related cough may take six to eight weeks to improve with PPI therapy, and asthma-related cough typically responds within four weeks of inhaled corticosteroid therapy. ACE inhibitor cough resolves in one to four weeks after stopping the offending drug.
complications: Rib fractures or costal cartilage injuries from forceful, repetitive coughing, especially in postmenopausal or osteoporotic individuals.
Urinary incontinence in women during severe coughing paroxysms due to sudden increases in intra-abdominal pressure.
Syncope (cough syncope) from transient reduction in cardiac output during prolonged coughing fits, particularly in men with COPD.
Pneumothorax (collapsed lung) from alveolar rupture during severe paroxysmal coughing, typically in patients with underlying emphysema.
Social isolation, depression, and markedly impaired quality of life associated with uncontrolled chronic cough, which affects sleep, exercise, and social activities.
Haemoptysis from mucosal trauma or an underlying lung lesion; any haemoptysis requires urgent investigation.

supportResources.name: American Lung Association — Cough Resources
supportResources.description: Patient-focused information on cough causes, diagnosis, and when to seek care from a leading U.S. respiratory health organisation.

supportResources.name: NHS — Cough
supportResources.description: Plain-English guidance on cough causes, self-care, and when to see a doctor from the UK National Health Service.

supportResources.name: CHEST Foundation — Chronic Cough
supportResources.description: Educational materials on chronic cough evaluation and management from the American College of Chest Physicians' patient foundation.

sources.title: Irwin RS et al. CHEST Expert Cough Panel Consensus Statement: Management of Cough as a Symptom in Adults and Children in the Primary Care Setting. Chest 2018
Gibson PG et al. ACCP Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis and Management of Chronic Cough. Chest 2006
Smith JA et al. Gefapixant for refractory or unexplained chronic cough (COUGH-1 and COUGH-2): two double-blind, parallel-group, randomised, placebo-controlled, phase 3 trials. Lancet 2022
Ryan NM et al. Gabapentin for refractory chronic cough: a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. Lancet 2012
NICE NG255 — Chronic cough in adults: assessment and management (2023)
Morice AH et al. ERS Guidelines on the Diagnosis and Treatment of Chronic Cough in Adults and Children. Eur Respir J 2020
Paul IM et al. Effect of Honey, Dextromethorphan, and No Treatment on Nocturnal Cough and Sleep Quality for Children. Arch Pediatr Adolesc Med 2007

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Typical asthma presents with wheeze, dyspnoea, and chest tightness alongside cough; cough-variant asthma causes isolated dry cough without wheeze but is confirmed by airway hyperresponsiveness on challenge testing.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: GERD-related cough is often dry, worsens after meals and when lying flat, and may occur with no heartburn at all; it is distinguished from infectious cough by its chronic course and absence of fever or purulent sputum.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TB cough is chronic, productive, and accompanied by night sweats, weight loss, and haemoptysis; it is confirmed by sputum smear, culture, or molecular testing, not by empirical treatment trials.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lung cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A new or changing cough in a smoker over 40, particularly with haemoptysis, weight loss, or a chest X-ray abnormality, mandates urgent CT and bronchoscopy to exclude malignancy before attributing cough to a benign cause.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Cough-variant asthma is one of the three commonest causes of chronic cough, and uncontrolled asthma perpetuates cough-reflex sensitisation.

relatedConditions.name: COPD
relatedConditions.relevance: COPD causes a chronic productive cough in most affected patients and is the principal long-term consequence of smoking-related cough.

relatedConditions.name: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Acid reflux triggers a vagally mediated cough reflex, accounting for up to 40% of chronic cough cases, often without heartburn symptoms.

relatedConditions.name: Pertussis (whooping cough)
relatedConditions.relevance: Pertussis causes severe paroxysmal coughing lasting weeks and is a preventable infectious cause of both subacute and chronic cough.

relatedConditions.name: Bronchiectasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Bronchiectasis causes a chronic daily productive cough from pooled secretions in dilated, damaged airways, often as a long-term consequence of recurrent respiratory infections.

faqs.question: When does a cough need medical attention?
faqs.answer: See a clinician if your cough lasts more than 3 weeks, if you cough up blood, if you have a high fever, chest pain, or unexplained weight loss, or if you have significant breathing difficulty. Any cough in a smoker over 40 that changes character warrants evaluation.

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of chronic cough?
faqs.answer: In adults who do not smoke and are not taking an ACE inhibitor, the three commonest causes are postnasal drip (upper-airway cough syndrome), cough-variant asthma, and acid reflux. Together these account for over 90% of chronic cough cases in primary care.

faqs.question: Can an ACE inhibitor cause a persistent cough?
faqs.answer: Yes. ACE inhibitors such as lisinopril and ramipril cause a dry, persistent cough in 10-15% of users by accumulating kinins in the airway. The cough usually starts within weeks of beginning the drug and resolves 1-4 weeks after stopping it. Switching to an angiotensin receptor blocker solves the problem without losing blood-pressure control.

faqs.question: How long does a cough from a cold last?
faqs.answer: A cough from a common cold typically peaks at 2-3 days and clears within 1-3 weeks. A cough that persists beyond 3 weeks after a respiratory illness is classed as subacute and may warrant assessment, particularly if it is worsening rather than gradually improving.

faqs.question: Does honey help with a cough?
faqs.answer: Yes, in children over 1 year of age. Multiple trials show that a teaspoon of honey at bedtime reduces nocturnal cough frequency and improves sleep at least as well as over-the-counter cough medicines. Honey should never be given to infants under 12 months due to the risk of infant botulism.

faqs.question: Can GERD cause a cough?
faqs.answer: Yes. Acid reflux triggers a cough reflex via the vagus nerve in up to 40% of chronic cough cases. Importantly, up to half of these patients have no heartburn at all, so GERD can be missed. An 8-week trial of a proton pump inhibitor twice daily, combined with dietary changes, is both diagnostic and therapeutic.

faqs.question: What makes a cough an emergency?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care for coughing up blood (more than a teaspoon), severe breathing difficulty, stridor, cyanosis (blue lips or nails), or sudden choking after eating — which may indicate foreign body aspiration. High fever with rapid breathing and one-sided chest pain suggests pneumonia or pulmonary embolism and also needs urgent evaluation.

faqs.question: Can coughing break a rib?
faqs.answer: Forceful or prolonged coughing can fracture ribs, particularly in women with osteoporosis or people on long-term corticosteroids. Sharp, localised chest wall pain that worsens with breathing or movement after a bout of severe coughing should be assessed with a chest X-ray.

faqs.question: What is cough-variant asthma?
faqs.answer: Cough-variant asthma is a form of asthma in which a dry, persistent cough is the only symptom — there is no wheeze or obvious shortness of breath. It accounts for 25-30% of chronic cough referrals. Spirometry may be normal; a methacholine challenge test is often needed to confirm airway hyperresponsiveness. It responds well to inhaled corticosteroids.

faqs.question: Is whooping cough still common in adults?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pertussis immunity from childhood vaccination wanes after 5-10 years, leaving adults susceptible. Adults typically experience a prolonged, paroxysmal cough with a characteristic whoop and post-cough vomiting lasting 6-10 weeks. A Tdap booster is recommended for adults who did not receive one in the last decade, especially those around infants.

faqs.question: Can smoking cause a chronic cough?
faqs.answer: Yes. A daily productive cough — smoker's cough — affects roughly 40-50% of long-term smokers and signals established airway disease including COPD. The cough typically improves significantly within 4-8 weeks of stopping smoking as cilia recover and mucus production decreases. Persistent cough after quitting still warrants chest X-ray to exclude malignancy.

faqs.question: What is postnasal drip and how does it cause cough?
faqs.answer: Postnasal drip occurs when excess mucus from inflamed nasal or sinus mucosa drips down the back of the throat, directly stimulating cough receptors. It is the leading cause of chronic cough and is most commonly associated with allergic rhinitis, sinusitis, or non-allergic rhinitis. Intranasal corticosteroids and antihistamines are the first-line treatment.

faqs.question: What is gefapixant and is it available?
faqs.answer: Gefapixant (Lyfnua) is a P2X3 receptor antagonist — the first drug approved specifically for chronic refractory cough. It blocks ATP-mediated nerve signalling in airway cough receptors and reduced cough frequency by about 40% versus placebo in phase 3 trials. It is approved in Europe and Japan; the main side effect is altered taste in about 40% of users.

faqs.question: Does a cough spread COVID-19 or other infections?
faqs.answer: Cough is a primary route of transmission for respiratory viruses including SARS-CoV-2, influenza, and SARS. Respiratory droplets and aerosols travel several metres during a cough. Covering the mouth and nose with a tissue or the elbow, washing hands, and wearing a mask in enclosed spaces reduces transmission risk significantly.

faqs.question: Can anxiety or stress cause a cough?
faqs.answer: Psychological factors can exacerbate or even initiate cough in some individuals through heightened laryngeal sensitivity and altered breathing patterns. Somatic cough disorder (formerly psychogenic cough) is a rare diagnosis of exclusion, but anxiety and stress can amplify perception of cough in people with an already sensitised cough reflex.

faqs.question: What is the cough reflex and how is it triggered?
faqs.answer: The cough reflex begins with activation of sensory receptors in the airways by irritants, mucus, or foreign material. These signals travel via the vagus nerve to the brainstem cough centre, which coordinates a deep breath, closure of the vocal cords, rapid pressure build-up, and explosive exhalation at speeds up to 500 km/h to clear the airways.

faqs.question: Should I take a cough suppressant or an expectorant?
faqs.answer: The choice depends on whether your cough is productive. A dry, non-productive cough may be eased by a suppressant (dextromethorphan or codeine-based), but these provide only modest short-term relief and are not recommended for children under 6 years. An expectorant such as guaifenesin helps thin mucus in productive cough. Neither replaces treating the underlying cause.

faqs.question: Can cough cause urinary leakage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Forceful coughing sharply raises intra-abdominal pressure, which can overcome the urethral sphincter in women with stress urinary incontinence. It affects up to 30% of women with chronic cough and is a major quality-of-life concern. Treating the cough and addressing the pelvic floor with physiotherapy both help.

faqs.question: Can a child's cough be a sign of whooping cough?
faqs.answer: Yes. A prolonged cough in a child featuring sudden paroxysms, a high-pitched inspiratory whoop, and vomiting after coughing fits strongly suggests pertussis, particularly if vaccination is incomplete or out of date. Infants under 3 months may have no whoop but can have life-threatening apnoea. A nasopharyngeal PCR test confirms the diagnosis.

faqs.question: What tests will a doctor do for a chronic cough?
faqs.answer: For a chronic cough lasting over 8 weeks, standard evaluation includes a chest X-ray, spirometry to check for asthma or COPD, and a review of all medications. If those are unrevealing, CT chest and sinuses and a PPI trial for reflux follow. A methacholine challenge test is used when cough-variant asthma is suspected with normal spirometry.

metaDescription: Cough affects 30 million U.S. clinic visits yearly. Learn the causes of acute and chronic cough, diagnostic tests, and treatments including new options for refractory cough.
